22: chasde YHWH ki lo tamnu ki lo chalu rachamav: 'The steadfast love [chesed] of the LORD never ceases; his mercies [rachamim] never come to an end.' This confession (Lam 3:22-24) is the theological center of Lamentations — placed at the exact midpoint of the book (the acrostic structure of ch. 3 is 22×3 = 66 verses, and v. 22 is the centerpiece). Its context makes it remarkable: Lamentations is the most sustained expression of grief and lamentation in Scripture, written in the immediate aftermath of Jerusalem's destruction, yet its center is a confession of divine steadfast love. The structure embodies the theology: suffering and praise, grief and faith, can coexist — and at the center of the darkest book is the brightest confession.
1: gever (H1397, man/warrior) opens the poem: ani hagever ra'ah 'oni beshevet 'evrato (I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his wrath). Gever derives from gabar (to be strong, to prevail, H1396) and denotes a man specifically in his capacity for strength or warrior identity — not merely ish (a man in relational/social terms) or adam (humanity in general). The poet identifies as the gever who has seen affliction — the strong man brought low, the warrior undone by the rod. The acrostic structure of ch. 3 (triple aleph-bet: each letter of the 22-letter Hebrew alphabet opens three consecutive verses) is the formal embodiment of the theological claim: grief has been given the most complete possible expression within ordered language. The discipline of the acrostic form holds the chaos of suffering within a structured container.
2: Oti nahag vayolek choshekh velo or (He has driven me and brought me into darkness and not light). The darkness/light antithesis (choshekh H2822 / or H216) recalls the creation vocabulary of Gen 1:2-4 — the primordial darkness before God's creative word. For the gever, YHWH's action has undone creation: instead of being led into light (the positive divine guidance of Ps 23:2-3), he is led into darkness. The inversion of Psalm 23's 'he leads me' is deliberate: Lamentations 3 is the anti-Psalm 23 before it becomes the psalm of trust again at vv 22-26. The verb nahag (to drive/lead, H5090) is used of shepherding livestock — YHWH as shepherd driving the flock into the dark.
3: Akh bi yashov yachpoch yado kol hayom (Only against me he turns; he turns his hand against me all day long). The repetition of yashov (he turns, from shub, H7725) — YHWH's hand turned against the poet repeatedly — picks up the shepherd-turning-against image. The akh (only/surely, H389) is a restrictive particle emphasizing the totality: not occasionally but exclusively, and not partially but with the whole hand. The three aleph-verses (1-3) establish the fullest possible lament: the man of strength, driven into darkness, beaten without relief.
4: Bilah besari ve'ori shavar atzmotai (He has made my flesh and my skin waste away; he has broken my bones). The bet-section (vv 4-6) moves to bodily suffering. Bilah (to waste away/wear out, H1086) is used of aged garments and old wineskins — the body worn out as fabric wears. Shavar (broken, H7665) for bones is the vocabulary of catastrophic physical force. Together flesh, skin, and bones represent the complete human body from outer to inner structure — the entire physical person destroyed. The vocabulary parallels Job's physical afflictions (Job 30:30; 33:21), situating Lamentations in the wider wisdom tradition of innocent suffering.
5: Banah alai vayaqef rosh vete'la (He has built against me and compassed me with bitterness/gall and hardship). The construction (banah, to build, H1129) against the poet uses siege-warfare language: YHWH is the siege engineer constructing fortifications against his own servant. Rosh (H7218, poison/gall, or possibly the poisonous plant Conium maculatum) and te'la (H8513, hardship/toil) represent the siege's contents. The same rosh appears in Lam 3:15, 19 and in Deuteronomy 29:18 as the 'root bearing poisonous fruit' — the language of covenant curse.
6: Bemakom chashekh hoshivani kemete 'olam (He has made me dwell in darkness like the dead of long ago). Chashekh (H4285, thick darkness, place of darkness) — here the abode of the dead, the darkness below. Mete 'olam (H4191 + H5769, the dead of old/ancient dead) — those who have been in Sheol for ages, completely forgotten. To be treated like the ancient dead is to be placed beyond the reach of any redemption, beyond the possibility of return — the poet's felt experience of total abandonment. The image anticipates 3:53-55 (they cast me into the pit).
7: Gadar ba'adi velo etze hikbid nechushtam (He has walled me in so I cannot get out; he has made my chain heavy). The gimel-section (vv 7-9) develops the siege/imprisonment imagery. Gadar (to wall, H1443) constructs an impenetrable barrier. The heavy chain (nechoshet, H5178, bronze/copper) — the metal of bondage (Ps 107:10: those in misery and iron chains) — is the physical embodiment of the impossibility of escape. The sequence of siege, darkness, chains forms the complete picture of imprisonment without exit.
8: Gam ki ez'aq vaashavea satar tefilati (Also when I cry out and shout, he shuts out my prayer). The most terrible element of the prison: prayer itself is blocked. Satar (to shut out/hide, H8168) applied to prayer is rare — it appears in Ps 22:2 (I cry out but you do not answer) in the same desperate context. The lament that prayer is unavailing — that the very channel of communication with YHWH has been blocked — is the depth of the theological crisis. The NT parallel: Christ's cry of dereliction (Matt 27:46) quotes Ps 22:1 in this same register of unanswered prayer under judgment.
9: Gadar derakhai begazit netivotai 'iqesh (He has walled in my paths with hewn stones; he has made my ways crooked). The gazit (hewn stone, H1496) — the dressed stone used in construction, including the temple — used here as blocking material. The paths are walled in with the very material of ordered civilized life. 'Iqesh (crooked, H5753, from 'awah, to make crooked) is the same root as the 'iniquity' word group — paths crooked/twisted, made inaccessible. The reversal of Isaiah 40:4's straight-path promise: there, valleys filled and crooked places made straight; here, straight paths are made crooked.
10: Dov orev hu li aryeh bemistarem (He is a bear lying in wait for me, a lion in hidden places). The dalet-section (vv 10-12) depicts YHWH as a predatory animal. The bear (dov, H1677) and the lion (aryeh, H738) are Israel's two most dangerous land predators — both are used in Hosea 13:7-8 for YHWH's judgment of Israel (I will be to them like a lion, like a bear robbed of her cubs). The image of YHWH as predator is one of the most shocking in the OT — it inverts the shepherd-protector image (Ps 23) to its opposite. The theological point: YHWH's opposition to his own people when they are under covenant curse is as inexorable as the attack of the most dangerous predator.
11: Derakhai sorer vayefashecheni shamani shomem (He turned aside my paths and tore me in pieces; he made me desolate). The tearing (pasach, H6582, hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible) applies the predator's action from v10: the bear/lion that was lying in wait now tears its prey. Shomem (desolate, H8074, from shamem) is the same word used for the desolation of Jerusalem and the temple — the poet's condition mirrors the city's. The rhyme of the words and the image creates a devastating compound: ambush, pursuit, destruction, desolation.
12: Darak kashto vayatziveni kematarah lachetz (He bent his bow and set me as a target for the arrow). The archery imagery (bow bent, archer's target) makes the violence even more deliberate: not the random violence of a hunting predator but the aimed, intentional violence of a skilled archer. YHWH as archer appears in Ps 7:12-13 and Job 16:12-13 — both in the context of innocent suffering. The matarah (H4307, target/mark) — the one deliberately aimed at — is the most isolated position possible: singled out for assault from a distance by a superior force.
13: Hevi bekilyotai benei ashpato (He has caused the arrows of his quiver to enter into my kidneys). The he-section (vv 13-15) focuses on the bodily impact of divine assault. Kilyot (H3629, kidneys, plural) — in Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys were the seat of deep emotion, conscience, and inner experience, comparable to the heart (lev). The kidneys are tested by YHWH (Ps 7:9; Jer 11:20; 17:10: I, YHWH, search the heart and test the kidneys) — to have YHWH's arrows entering the kidneys is for divine judgment to penetrate to the deepest seat of personhood.
14: Hayiti sehoq lekhol ami neglnato kol hayom (I have become the laughingstock of all peoples, their taunt song all day long). The public mockery (sehoq, H7814, laughter/mockery) — the person of God as the object of communal derision. The neginah (H5058, taunt-song, song of mockery) — a musical form that twists praise into contempt. The same vocabulary appears in Job 30:9 (and now they mock me in song; I am a byword to them) and in Ps 69:12 (those who sit in the gate talk about me, and I am the song of drunkards). The mock-song directed at the individual or the city is the public face of covenant curse.
15: Hisbiani bamerorim hirvani la'anah (He has filled me with bitterness; he has made me drunk with wormwood). Merorim (H4844, bitter things, from marar to be bitter) is the word for the bitter herbs of the Passover meal (Exod 12:8; Num 9:11) — the Passover memory of slavery now inverted into present suffering. La'anah (H3939, wormwood, Artemisia absinthium) is the bitter plant that symbolizes calamity and judgment throughout the prophets (Jer 9:15; 23:15; Amos 5:7; 6:12 — turning justice into wormwood). To be drunk on wormwood is to be saturated with the substance of covenant curse.
16: Vayyagreis bekhatsats shinai hikpi'ani va'efer (He has broken my teeth with gravel; he has made me cower in ashes). The vav-section (vv 16-18) moves to the deepest despair. Khatsats (H2687, gravel/small stones) — the sensation of biting down on grit, the destruction of the ability to eat. Hikpi'ani (he has made me grovel/cower, from kafah H3721, hapax — to press down, bend low) combined with efer (H665, ashes) — the gesture of mourning, prostration in ashes (Job 2:8; Gen 18:27: 'I who am but dust and ashes'). The ashes are both the mourning gesture and the result of the destruction — the city has become ashes.
17: Vatizneach min-shalom nafshi nashiti tovah (And you have removed my soul from peace; I have forgotten prosperity). Nefesh (H5315, soul/life/self — the whole living person, not the Greek-influenced disembodied soul) is here the subject of displacement: the whole living self has been removed from shalom (H7965, peace, wholeness, the comprehensive well-being of covenant blessing). The translation notes flag the nefesh polysemy: 'soul' (Greek-influenced) vs. 'life' (the whole living person). The forgetting of tov (goodness/prosperity, H2896) — the created goodness that YHWH called good in Gen 1 — represents the total negation of the creation-blessing.
18: Va'omar avad nitzchi vitochalti min-YHWH (And I said, My endurance has perished, and my hope from the LORD). The nitzchi (H5331, my endurance/perpetuity, from netzach) — the word used for 'forever' in the lament psalms (Ps 13:1: How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?). The explicit naming of YHWH as the one from whom hope has been cut is the crisis: the very source of hope is experienced as the cause of hopelessness. The verse is the nadir before the great turn of vv 21-24. The structure is deliberate: the poet states the absolute loss of hope in v18 precisely because what follows (vv 21-24) is the recovery of hope — hope that is real only because it has passed through this depth.
19: Zechor 'onyi umrudi la'anah varo'sh (Remember my affliction and my wandering, the wormwood and the gall). The zayin-section (vv 19-21) is the pivot: from the deepest despair to renewed hope. The call to remember (zakar, H2142) — directed first at the poet himself (vv 19-21) — is the OT's primary mechanism for theological reorientation. Memory in the Hebrew tradition is not passive recollection but active engagement that produces new action. The pairing of la'anah (wormwood) and rosh (gall/poison) returns to the bitterness vocabulary of vv 5, 15 — but now in the context of the zayin-section which will move toward hope.
20: Zakhor tizkor vetashokh 'alay nafshi (My soul continually remembers it and is bowed down within me). The unusual doubled verb form zakhor tizkor (remembering it remembers — the infinitive absolute + finite verb construction, emphatic repetition) emphasizes the relentless quality of memory: it will not stop. The nefesh (soul/life-self) bowed down within — the inward posture of prostration. This verse stands between despair and hope: the memory that prostrates is the same memory that, when turned in the right direction (v21: 'this I recall to my heart'), produces hope. Memory is not inherently therapeutic or destructive — its direction determines its effect.
21: Zot ashiv el libbi 'al ken 'ochal (This I call to mind, and therefore I have hope). The pivot verse: ashiv (I will return, from shub, to turn/return, H7725) — the same verb used for repentance and turning in the prophets. The poet turns his attention (the lev/heart-mind) to the zot (this) — what follows in vv 22-24, the chesed of YHWH. The entire depression of vv 1-20 is reframed by a deliberate act of theological memory: 'this I call to mind.' Hope (yachal, H3176, to wait/hope/expect) returns not as a feeling that arose spontaneously but as the result of a deliberate cognitive act of remembering the character of YHWH. This is the OT's most concentrated statement of the mechanics of hope under suffering.
23: Chadashim labeqarim rabbah emunatecha (They are new every morning; great is your faithfulness). The renewal formula: morning (boqer, H1242) as the recurring renewal of YHWH's mercies — each morning is a new bestowal of the steadfast love declared in v22. Emunah (H530, faithfulness, from aman to be firm/reliable) — the word underlying 'Amen'; it denotes the solid reliability of YHWH's character. Rabbah emunatecha (great is your faithfulness) — the exclamation that stands on its own as theological praise in the middle of the lament. The morning renewal of mercy is not cyclical vanity (as Qohelet's sun-rises-and-sets is) but the repeated bestowal of the same faithful love — new in each application, same in its source.
24: Chelqi YHWH amar nafshi 'al ken 'ochal lo (My portion is the LORD, says my soul, therefore I will hope in him). Chelek (H2506, portion/share/inheritance) is the OT's covenant-distribution term: each tribe received its chelek in the land; the Levites' portion was YHWH himself (Deut 10:9; 18:2). To claim YHWH as one's chelek when everything material has been destroyed — when the land, the temple, the city, the society have all been stripped away — is the radical form of the covenant statement: the relationship with YHWH outlasts every temporal holding. The translation notes flag YHWH: the divine personal name (Yahweh), typically rendered LORD in small caps but hiding the personal name.
25: Tov YHWH le'kov lo lenafs tiddreshenu (The LORD is good to those who wait for him, to the soul that seeks him). The tet-section (vv 25-27) develops the waiting theology established in v24. Tov YHWH (the LORD is good) — the foundation for waiting: goodness is the reliable character that makes waiting rational rather than absurd. Qavah (to wait/hope, H6960, from a root meaning to twist together like a cord) — the expectation of a person who is straining toward the fulfillment of a promise. The soul (nefesh) that seeks YHWH (darash, H1875, to seek/inquire/resort to) — the active posture of seeking, not passive resignation.
26: Tov vedumam leyesha' YHWH savah veyikhol (It is good that one should wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD). Dumam (silently/quietly, H1748) — the silence of patient waiting, from damam (to be silent, to be still, H1826; cf. Ps 62:1: For God alone my soul waits in silence). Yeshua (H3444, salvation/deliverance) — the feminine form of the proper name Jesus (Yeshua, H3091). The verse declares that waiting silently for the yeshua of YHWH is itself the good posture — the form of faith that the crisis requires. The name Jesus embedded in the Hebrew word for salvation is one of the canonical textual connections between the OT's vocabulary and the NT's person.
27: Tov legever ki yissa' ol bine'urav (It is good for a man to bear the yoke in his youth). The gever from v1 returns: the man of strength bearing the yoke. 'Ol (H5923, yoke) — the instrument of labor and submission. In youth (bine'urav, in his young years) — the earlier the discipline is learned, the better. The same verb nasa (to bear/carry, H5375) is used for bearing sin, bearing the ark, bearing the covenant obligations — the yoke is not merely physical labor but the discipline of covenant obligation and the acceptance of one's condition under YHWH's sovereignty. Jeremiah's sign-act of wearing a yoke (Jer 27-28) is the enacted version of this call.
28: Yeshev badad veyiddom ki natal alav (Let him sit alone in silence when he has laid it on him). The yod-section (vv 28-30) describes the posture of the one under divine discipline. Badad (H910, alone/isolation) — the same word used for Jerusalem's isolation in Lam 1:1 (how she sits alone). The solitude of the one bearing the yoke is the structural solitude of the city — both the poet and the city are alone, silent, enduring. Dumam (silence) returns from v26 — the silence before YHWH as the appropriate response to what YHWH has laid on. The willingness to sit alone in silence rather than resist or protest is the form of acceptance the text commends.
29: Yitten be'afar pihu ulay yesh tiqvah (Let him put his mouth in the dust — there may yet be hope). The gesture of putting one's mouth in the dust (afar, H6083, dust — the death-material of Gen 3:19: you are dust) is the maximum gesture of submission and humiliation before a superior. Abraham in Gen 18:27 identifies himself as 'dust and ashes'; Job in 42:6 repents 'in dust and ashes'. The posture is not self-abasement for its own sake but the recognition of creaturely finitude before the Creator — and out of that recognition, the possibility of hope (tiqvah, H8615): 'there may yet be hope.' The hope that emerges from the dust-posture is the hope that comes after the self-assertion has been completely abandoned.
30: Yitten lemakkehu lehi yisba' becherpa (Let him give his cheek to the one who strikes, and let him be filled with insults). The cheek-turning under assault (malah, H3895, cheek/jaw) — the willingness to absorb assault without retaliation, presented here as the form of submission under discipline. This is the OT's most direct parallel to Jesus's teaching about turning the other cheek (Matt 5:39) and to Isaiah 50:6 (the Servant: I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard). The one who absorbs insult and assault in the context of bearing the yoke participates in the pattern of the Servant's submission.
31: Ki lo yiznak leolam Adonai (For the Lord will not cast off forever). The kaf-section (vv 31-33) provides the theological basis for the endurance called for in vv 28-30. Zanach (H2186, to cast away/reject, H2186) + le'olam (H5769, forever/for an age) — YHWH's rejection is not permanent. The translation notes flag 'olam: 'eternal/everlasting/age-long' — the duration is emphatic but limited (will not cast off le'olam = for the long term, but not permanently). Adonai (H136, my lord, used as the substitute for YHWH in liturgical reading) — this verse uses Adonai rather than YHWH, consistent with ch. 3's shift from YHWH (personal name) to Adonai (title) in the central section.
32: Ki im hogah veraham kerov chasadav (But though he causes grief, he will have compassion according to the abundance of his steadfast love). The chesed (H2617, steadfast love) from v22 returns: the abundant chesed is the theological ground for the 'not forever' of v31. The structure of v32: the grief is real and caused by YHWH (ki im hogah = though/when he causes grief) — no denial of the suffering — but the compassion (raham, from rachamim, womb-love, the deepest tenderness) comes from the abundance (H7230, rob) of his chesed. The quantitative qualifier (abundance, rob) stresses that YHWH's steadfast love is not a thin trickle of mercy but an overflowing resource.
33: Ki lo innah velibo venoga beney ish (For he does not afflict from his heart or grieve the children of men). Innah (H6031, to afflict/humble) from his heart (libo, his heart) — YHWH does not afflict willingly, from his core desire. The affliction that characterizes the entire poem is not YHWH's positive will but his reluctant judicial action; his heart desires compassion rather than judgment. This is the OT's most explicit statement that divine judgment is always instrumental (for purposes of restoration, correction, refinement) rather than expressive (of YHWH's pleasure in punishment). James 2:13 — mercy triumphs over judgment — is the NT's form of this principle.
34: Lakadkad tachat ragluv kol asirei aretz (To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the earth). The lamed-section (vv 34-36) continues the theological reflection on what YHWH does not approve. These three verses are list of injustices that, the text implies, YHWH sees and does not approve: crushing prisoners (v34), subverting justice before the Most High (v35), defrauding a man in court (v36). The theological point: the suffering of ch. 3 is not the result of YHWH approving these injustices. The very God who appears to have been acting unjustly (according to vv 1-20) is in fact the God who opposes the crushing of prisoners.
35: Lehattot mishpat gever lifnei penei Elyon (To subvert the right of a man before the face of the Most High). Mishpat (H4941, justice/right/judgment) — the legal right of the individual. Elyon (H5945, Most High) — the divine title expressing YHWH's sovereignty over all human courts. To subvert justice in the presence of the Most High — who sees all human proceedings — is to defy the one whose own character is justice. The three-verse sequence (34-36) uses the infinitive construct (to crush, to subvert, to deny) as legal charges against the oppressor, not against YHWH.
36: Leavet adam berivov Adonai lo ra'ah (To deny a man justice in his lawsuit — the Lord does not approve). Adon lo ra'ah (the Lord does not approve/see) — the final declaration that YHWH does not sanction injustice. This verse is the theological pivot: YHWH who appeared to be acting against the poet (vv 1-20) is in fact the God who opposes unjust treatment. The poet's suffering is not YHWH approving his oppression but YHWH's judicial action in the context of covenant discipline — a different category from the injustice YHWH will not sanction.
37: Mi zeh amar vatahi Adonai lo tzivah (Who is he who speaks and it comes to pass unless the Lord has commanded it?). The mem-section (vv 37-39) draws out the theological implications of the previous section. Amar vatahi (speaks and it comes to pass) — the efficacy of the divine word from Gen 1 (God said, and it was). The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Nothing comes to pass that YHWH has not commanded — including the suffering of ch. 3. The theodicy is not resolved (why did YHWH command this?) but the sovereignty is affirmed (nothing happens outside YHWH's authority).
38: Mippi Elyon lo tetzeh hara'ot vehatov (Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that good and evil go forth?). The good-and-evil-from-God statement is the most directly theodicy-engaging verse in the chapter. Ra'ot (evil/calamity, H7451) and hatov (the good, H2896) both proceeding from the mouth of Elyon. This is not a metaphysical claim that YHWH is the author of moral evil but the covenantal claim that both covenant blessing and covenant curse come from YHWH's sovereign word (cf. Isa 45:7: I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity — I am the LORD who does all these things). The suffering of Lamentations is the ra'ah of covenant curse, which YHWH commands in Deuteronomy 28 for covenant violation.
39: Mah yitonen adam chai gever al chata'av (Why should a living man complain, a man, about the punishment of his sins?). The rhetorical question — why should a gever (the powerful man of v1, now humbled) complain about the punishment for his sins? — is the response to the implied complaint of the entire chapter. The living man (chai) who still lives under punishment has the capacity and opportunity for repentance; the gever who complains about justified punishment misunderstands his situation. The verse functions as a rebuke to self-pity and a call to accountability — and sets up the call to repentance in vv 40-42.
40: Nachpesah derachenu venachqorah venashuvah ad YHWH (Let us search out our ways and examine them, and turn again to the LORD). The nun-section (vv 40-42) moves from individual theodicy to communal repentance. Chafas (H2664, to search/examine) + chaqar (H2713, to examine closely/investigate) — the double verb of thorough self-examination. Shub (H7725, to return/turn) ad YHWH — the standard OT vocabulary of repentance: turning back to the covenant God. The translation notes flag YHWH as the divine personal name. The communal 'we' replaces the individual 'I' — the poet's suffering has become the community's call.
41: Nissa levavenu el kapayim el El bashamayim (Let us lift up our hearts with our hands to God in heaven). Nissa levavenu (lift up our hearts) — prayer is not merely the uplifted hands but the uplifted heart-mind (lev, H3820). The combination of heart and hands in prayer — inner orientation and outward gesture combined — represents the whole-person engagement that genuine prayer requires. El bashamayim (God in the heavens, H410 + H8064) — the transcendent God who is simultaneously the personal covenant God. The heavenward direction acknowledges that the solution must come from beyond the horizontal plane of human action.
42: Nachnu fashanu umarimu veatah lo salachtah (We have transgressed and rebelled, and you have not forgiven). The brutally honest confession: fashanu (we have transgressed, from pesha, H6586, rebellion/transgression — the strongest sin vocabulary: willful defiance, breach of relationship) + marimu (we have rebelled, from marah, H4784, to be rebellious/disobedient). The second half is equally brutal: veatah lo salachtah (and you have not forgiven). The confession does not end with the expectation of immediate forgiveness — it holds the acknowledgment of sin and the experience of unresolved judgment together without collapsing one into the other. This is the most honest form of confession: true acknowledgment that does not presuppose its acceptance.
43: Sakotah va'af vatirdefenu haragta velo chamaltah (You have wrapped yourself with anger and pursued us; you have killed and have not pitied). The samech-section (vv 43-45) is the communal lament resumed. Sakotah (you have covered/wrapped yourself, from sakak, H5526) with anger (af, H639) — YHWH clothed in wrath, surrounded by it. The pursuit and killing without pity is the language of Deuteronomy 28's covenant curse fulfilled: the curses listed in Deut 28:15-68 include precisely this relentless pursuit and destruction without mercy (Deut 28:65-67). Lamentations is the experiential form of what Deuteronomy threatened.
44: Sakotah be'anan lecha me'avor tefillah (You have covered yourself with a cloud so that no prayer can pass through). The cloud (anan, H6051) as the barrier to prayer — inverting the cloud's normal OT function. In the wilderness, the cloud was the symbol of YHWH's guiding, protective presence (Exod 13:21; 40:34-35); here the cloud is the barrier between the people and YHWH. The protective cloud has become the blocking cloud — the symbol of divine presence has become the symbol of divine hiddenness. The only way through the cloud-barrier is the one who is himself the divine presence: Christ as the one through whom prayer reaches the Father (John 14:6, 13-14).
45: Siach usma'os tesimenu bekerev ha'amim (You have made us scum and garbage among the peoples). Siach (H5501, offscouring/rubbish) and ma'os (H3973, refuse/rejection) — the two strongest contempt-vocabulary words in the OT. Paul uses a Greek equivalent in 1 Cor 4:13: 'we have become the scum of the world, the refuse of all things' — applying the Lamentations vocabulary to the apostolic experience of suffering for the gospel. The community at the bottom of the social hierarchy, treated as refuse — this is the community that receives the beatitudes ('blessed are the poor in spirit'; 'blessed are those who are persecuted').
46: Patzenu aleinu pihem kol oyveynu (All our enemies have opened their mouths against us). The ayin-section (vv 46-48) focuses on the enemies who take advantage of the city's collapse. The opened mouth (patzah, H6473, to gape/open wide) of the enemies — the gloating, the taunting, the triumphant abuse of the defeated. The same imagery appears in Ps 22:13: roaring lions that open their mouths wide. The enemies' open mouths contrast with the blocked prayer of v44 — the enemies have voice while YHWH's people are silenced.
47: Pachad vefachat hayah lanu hashait vehasshever (Panic and pitfall have come upon us, devastation and destruction). The four-word sequence (pachad H6343 panic, pachath H6354 pit/trap, shait H7722 devastation, shever H7667 fracture/destruction) uses the literary device of consonance — the ph/sh sounds create a rushing, overwhelming sequence. The pit (pachath) picks up the pit imagery of vv 52-55. The combination of trap and destruction is the vocabulary of Isa 24:17-18 (the three-fold curse: pachad, pachath, pakh — panic, pit, trap) — Lamentations 3 is Deuteronomy's covenant curse and Isaiah's universal judgment concentrated in the singular experience of the poet.
48: Palgei mayim tored 'eini 'al shever bat 'ammi (My eye runs down with rivers of tears because of the destruction of the daughter of my people). Palgei mayim (H6388 + H4325, streams/channels of water) — the eye as a river source, the weeping as uncontrollable as a flowing stream. Bat 'ammi (H1323 + H5971, daughter of my people) — the personification of the community as a daughter, consistent with the 'daughter of Zion' language throughout Lamentations. The weeping over the destruction of the community connects the individual lament (the gever) to the communal suffering that the whole book mourns.
49: Eini nezalah velo tidmeh me'ein haftugot (My eye pours down without ceasing, without respite). The pe-section (vv 49-51) intensifies the weeping. Nizalah (poured down, from nazal, H5140, to flow/drip) — continuous flow without interruption. Me'ein haftugot (without any ceasing, from hafag, H6315, to cease/stop — another hapax in this form) — the utter absence of relief from weeping. The weeping without ceasing connects to Jeremiah's own weeping (Jer 9:1: Oh that my head were waters, and my eyes a fountain of tears) — the prophet who saw the disaster weeping over it without relief.
50: Ad yashqif veyire YHWH mishshamayim (Until the LORD looks down and sees from heaven). The 'until' (ad, H5704) is the hinge of the entire weeping sequence: the weeping continues without ceasing until YHWH acts. The looking down from heaven (shaqaf, H8259, to look down/lean out) — the same word used of YHWH looking down from heaven in Ps 14:2 and 102:20 (YHWH looked down from his holy height; from heaven he looked at the earth to hear the groans of the prisoners). The appeal to YHWH's seeing from heaven presupposes that YHWH will respond when he sees — the theodicy of faith that his seeing leads to acting.
51: Eini 'ollela lenefshy mikol benot 'iri (My eye affects/grieves my soul because of all the daughters of my city). 'Ollela (H5953, to deal severely with/to afflict, from 'alal, to deal harshly) — the eye's witnessing of destruction itself produces grief in the nefesh. 'All the daughters of my city' (benot 'iri) — the plural 'daughters' as the communities/neighborhoods of Jerusalem, each representing a population affected. The poet's own nefesh (total self) is damaged by what the eyes have seen — the psychological reality of vicarious suffering, anticipating Ezekiel's 'I sat where they sat' (Ezek 3:15).
52: Tzod tzaduni kaytzippor oyevai chinnam (My enemies hunted me like a bird without cause). The tsade-section (vv 52-54) narrates the poet's experience of the pit. Tzad (to hunt, H6679) — used of hunting animals for food. Kaytzippor (like a bird, H6833) — the vulnerability of the hunted bird, caught in a snare (cf. Ps 124:7: our soul has escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowler). Chinnam (without cause, H2600) — the same word Ps 35:7, 19 use for David's enemies: they hid a net without cause; let them not rejoice who hate me without cause. The 'without cause' invokes the category of innocent suffering: the poet's hunting is not punishment for specific guilt but the unjust assault of enemies.
53: Tzamtu vavor chayyai vayyiddyu even bi (They have cut off my life in the pit and cast stones on me). Bor (H953, pit/cistern/dungeon) — one of the most theologically laden words in the OT: Joseph was thrown into a bor by his brothers (Gen 37:20-29); Jeremiah was thrown into a bor with mud (Jer 38:6); Ps 28:1 and 30:3 use the bor for Sheol, the realm of the dead. The pit is simultaneously a physical imprisonment and a figure for death and Sheol. The stone-throwing (yaddyu even) on the person in the pit recalls the stoning of prisoners. The combination of pit-imprisonment and stone-throwing creates the image of sealed entombment.
54: Tzafu mayim 'al roshi amarti nighzarti (The waters flowed over my head; I said, I am cut off). The flood-over-the-head image — drowning, overwhelmed by waters. Nighzarti (I am cut off, from gazar, H1504, to cut/divide) — the finality of the declaration. This is the deepest point of the poem: entombed in the pit, flooded by waters, the poet declares himself cut off. The same moment of apparent finality appears in Ps 31:22: 'I said in my alarm, I am cut off from your sight.' The declaration of being cut off is immediately followed by the appeal to YHWH (Ps 31:22b; Lam 3:55) — the moment of utter hopelessness is the moment of crying out.
55: Qarati shimcha YHWH mibor tachtioth (I called on your name, O LORD, from the depths of the pit). The qof-section (vv 55-57) is the turning point: from the pit, crying to YHWH. Bor tachtioth (H953 + H8482, the lowest pit) — the double intensification: the pit, and the lowest part of the pit. The divine name (shimcha YHWH, your name, YHWH) is invoked from the maximum depth — the same structure as Jonah 2 (from the belly of Sheol, Jonah prays and YHWH hears). The prayer from the pit to YHWH is the climax and reversal of v44 (your cloud blocks prayer) — what seemed impossible (prayer getting through the cloud) is here enacted: the poet prays from the pit and YHWH will answer.
56: Qoli shama'ta al ta'alem oznicha leravchati leshav'ati (You have heard my voice; do not close your ear to my plea, to my cry for relief). The shift to the past tense assertion — qoli shama'ta (my voice, you heard it) — represents the rhetorical shift from petition to confidence: YHWH has already heard. Revach (H7305, relief/space/release, a hapax here) — the breathing-room of relief after constriction. The plea not to close the ear (al ta'alem) grounds the confidence in YHWH's character: the one who heard in the past will not close his ear in the future. The past hearing becomes the basis for present confidence.
57: Qaravta beyom eqra'eka amarta al tira (You came near on the day I called to you; you said, Do not fear). Qaravta (you came near, from qarav, H7126) — YHWH's drawing near in response to the cry. The divine assurance al tira (do not fear, H3372) — the most frequent divine speech act in the OT (appearing over 70 times). YHWH's nearness and his 'fear not' together represent the complete resolution of the crisis: the far God who covered himself with a cloud is now near; the terrifying God is the one who says 'do not fear.' This is the pattern of every theophany: approach produces fear; YHWH's address removes the fear.
58: Ravta Adonai rivei nafshi ga'alta chayyai (You have taken up my cause, O Lord; you have redeemed my life). The resh-section (vv 58-60). Rivah (to contend/plead the case, H7378) — legal terminology: YHWH as the advocate who pleads the case. Ga'alta chayyai (you have redeemed my life, from ga'al, H1350, the kinsman-redeemer) — the go'el vocabulary: the one who redeems what was forfeit or imprisoned by acting as the nearest kin. The go'el relationship (Boaz for Ruth; Job's living Redeemer, Job 19:25) here becomes YHWH's action for the poet: the one in the pit has been redeemed. The translation notes flag nefesh: 'my life/my self', the whole living person rather than a Greek-influenced immaterial soul.
59: Ra'ita YHWH 'avvatti shofta mishpati (You have seen, O LORD, my wrong; judge my cause). The appeal to YHWH as judge after he has redeemed (v58) — the redemption is the basis for the appeal. 'Avvatti (my wrong/injustice suffered, from 'awah, H5753, to act wrongly; here the wrong done to the poet) — the injustice that was perpetrated against him. Shofta mishpati (judge my cause) — the call for YHWH to act as judge on behalf of the one he has redeemed. The redeemed person still awaits vindication; the double act (redemption from the pit; judgment that vindicates) are distinguished.
60: Ra'ita kol nikmataim kol machshevetam li (You have seen all their vengeance, all their plots against me). The knowledge that YHWH has seen the enemies' plans is itself a form of comfort: the suffering was not invisible or ignored. Nikmah (H5360, vengeance/retaliation) — YHWH as the one who sees and will act. Machshevetam (their plots/plans, from chashav, H2803, to think/plan) — the deliberate, premeditated nature of the enemies' assault. The poet's appeal rests on the fact that YHWH sees the injustice — the witnessing God who will act as judge.
61: Shama'ta cherpatam YHWH kol machshevetam 'alay (You have heard their taunts, O LORD, all their plots against me). The shin-section (vv 61-63) repeats the appeal to YHWH's perception: heard and seen (v60-61). Cherpatam (H2781, their insults/reproaches) — the public shame instrument, the insult directed at a person to destroy their social standing. YHWH has heard even the private plots and the public taunts — the comprehensive witness of the divine observer who misses nothing. The appeal to YHWH's comprehensive knowledge counters the experience of abandonment: YHWH was not absent but fully present as witness even when he seemed absent as deliverer.
62: Siftei qamay vehegeyonam 'alay kol hayom (The lips of those who rise against me and their whispering against me all day long). The enemies' lips (safah, H8193) and their murmuring/whispering (haggayon, H1902, meditation/murmuring) all day long — the constant, sustained verbal assault. The 'all day long' (kol hayom) has appeared multiple times in ch. 3 as the emphasis on the relentlessness of the suffering: all day the hand turned against him (v3), all day the mockery (v14), all day the scheming (v62). The repetition creates the texture of unrelenting assault that has no pause or relief.
63: Shivtam vequmam habbitah ani neginataim (Behold their sitting and rising; I am their mocking song). Shivtam uqumam (their sitting and rising) — the idiom for 'all their activities at all times' (cf. Deut 6:7: when you sit and when you rise). The poet is the subject of their mockery across all their activities, from sitting to rising. Neginatam (their mocking song, H5058 — the same word as v14's taunt-song) — the full circle: the enemies have made the poet the subject of their musical mockery from morning to night, in every posture.
64: Tashiv lahem gemul YHWH kema'aseh yedeihem (Give them their due, O LORD, according to the work of their hands). The tav-section (vv 64-66) is the imprecatory close — the call for divine justice against the enemies. Gemul (H1576, recompense/payment, from gamal, to deal out/repay) — the lex talionis principle: let what they did be done to them. The appeal is to YHWH as the judge who enforces the moral order: the one who sees (vv 59-63) will also act. The imprecatory prayer is grounded in confidence in divine justice rather than personal revenge.
65: Titten lahem meginnat lev ta'alatcha lahem (Give them hardness of heart, your curse for them). Meginnat lev (hardness/stubbornness of heart, H4044 + H3820 — a rare compound) — the closing up of the heart that prevents repentance, the judicial hardening that YHWH applies to those whose wickedness is complete (cf. Exod 4:21; Isa 63:17). Ta'alatcha (your curse, H8381, from ta'ala, to curse — a rare word) — the formal covenant curse invoked against those who have violated covenant. The double request (hardness of heart + curse) represents the complete shut-off of possibility: no repentance, and the curse fully in force.
66: Tirdof be'af vetashmid mitachat shemei YHWH (Pursue them in anger and destroy them from under your heavens, O LORD). The closing verse returns to the language of pursuit (radaf, H7291, to chase/pursue — used of YHWH pursuing Israel in judgment, Deut 28:45, and now invoked against Israel's enemies). Mitachat shemei YHWH (from under the heavens of YHWH) — the destruction is complete: the enemies are removed from the whole created order that YHWH governs. The poem ends not with comfort but with the confident invocation of divine judgment — the trust that YHWH will vindicate having been established in vv 21-60, the closing call for judgment expresses that same trust in its judicial form.
